title: The .then() Method 🔗
description: Chain multiple actions together as soon as a Future completes.

slides.title: The Alternative to Await 🛤️
slides.content:
# Chaining Futures

`await` forces your function to pause and wait for the box to open.
But what if you DON'T want your function to pause? You want it to finish immediately, and just tell Dart: *"Hey, whenever that box finally opens, run this other code."*

You can use the `.then()` method!

slides.title: Then Do This 👉

quizData.question: What is the primary difference between `await` and `.then()`?

options.text: They do exactly the same thing.
`await` pauses the current function until the data arrives. `.then()` allows the function to continue instantly, scheduling the code to run later when the data arrives.
`.then()` crashes if there is an error.

quizData.explanation: Await is synchronous-style phrasing. Then() is callback-style phrasing.

slides.title: Catching Errors 💥
slides.content:
## `.catchError()`

If you use `await`, you trap errors using a standard `try / catch` block.
If you use `.then()`, you trap errors by chaining `.catchError()` right onto the end!

```dart
downloadAvatar()
  .then((img) => print('Got image!'))
  .catchError((error) => print('Failed to download: $error'));
```
This is a very clean way to perform background tasks without stopping your UI.

quizData.question:
Fill in the blank to log a failure when chaining futures:
api.login().then((user) => save()).________((e) => log(e));
quizData.explanation: The specific method chained to a Future to handle its failure is .catchError()
